_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows David Eck's Introduction to Programming Using Java. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the source guides curriculum order only.
title: Maps
summary: Look up values by a meaningful key instead of by position.
refresher: A Java `HashMap` connects keys to values. Use `put` to store a pair, `get` to look up a value, and `containsKey` when a missing key needs special handling. The key type and value type go inside angle brackets.

blocks.body: A list is great when position matters. A map is better when a meaningful key matters. If you want a grade by student id, the student id should be the key.

blocks.caption: Declare variables with the Map interface and construct a HashMap. Each `put` connects one key to one value.
blocks.body: HashMap is Java's everyday map. It stores key-value pairs. The first type in angle brackets is the key type. The second type is the value type.
Keys are unique. Putting `CS101` again replaces its earlier value instead of creating a duplicate entry. Different keys may still store equal values.
blocks.caption: put stores the pair. get uses the key to find the value.
`get` returns null for a missing key, which can cause trouble when Java unboxes it to int. `getOrDefault` makes the absent case explicit.
blocks.body: A missing key returns `null`. That is not always an error, but it is something you must plan for. Use `containsKey` when you need to know whether the key was present.

blocks.title: Null does not prove a key is absent
blocks.body: A map is allowed to store null as a value. If that distinction matters, use `containsKey` to ask whether the key exists rather than guessing from `get`.

blocks.why: Both calls use the same key, so the second call replaces its value. One mapping remains, and its current value is 4.

blocks.body: Use `entrySet()` when a loop needs keys and values together. Each entry provides `getKey()` and `getValue()` without a second lookup.

blocks.prompt: Which view gives both keys and values for a loop?
blocks.why: `entrySet()` contains Map.Entry objects, and every entry exposes both sides of the mapping. The other views provide only keys or only values.

blocks.title: Map checklist
items: Use a map when lookup by name or id matters.
Use put to store a key-value pair.
Use get to read by key.
Check containsKey before treating a missing value as real data.
Pick clear key and value types.
